mcqs.question: Which of the following best describes the class P?

options.text: Problems that can be solved in polynomial time
Problems that can be verified in polynomial time
Problems that cannot be solved in polynomial time
Problems that cannot be verified in polynomial time

mcqs.explanation: P consists of decision problems that can be solved by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.

mcqs.question: What is the relationship between P and NP?

options.text: P = NP
P ⊆ NP
NP ⊆ P
P and NP are disjoint

mcqs.explanation: Every problem in P is also in NP because if we can solve a problem in polynomial time, we can certainly verify a solution in polynomial time.

mcqs.question: Which problem was the first to be proven NP-complete?

options.text: Traveling Salesman Problem
Boolean Satisfiability (SAT)
Hamiltonian Path
Graph Coloring

mcqs.explanation: Stephen Cook proved in 1971 that the Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT) is NP-complete, providing the first example of an NP-complete problem.

mcqs.question: What does it mean for a problem to be NP-hard?

options.text: It is in NP and verifiable in polynomial time
It is at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP
It can be solved in polynomial time
It cannot be verified in polynomial time

mcqs.explanation: A problem is NP-hard if every problem in NP can be reduced to it in polynomial time, meaning it is at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP.

mcqs.question: If a polynomial-time algorithm is discovered for an NP-complete problem, what would be the consequence?

options.text: P would equal NP
All NP problems would become solvable in polynomial time
The problem would no longer be NP-complete
All of the above

mcqs.explanation: If any NP-complete problem could be solved in polynomial time, then all problems in NP could be solved in polynomial time (P = NP), and the problem would no longer be considered NP-complete since it would be in P.
